name: nutree

metadata.name: nutree
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules
metadata.summary: A Python library for tree data structures with an intuitive, yet powerful, API.

documentation:
# ![logo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mar10/nutree/main/docs/nutree_48x48.png) nutree [![Latest Version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/nutree.svg)](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/nutree/) [![Tests](https://github.com/mar10/nutree/actions/workflows/tests.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/mar10/nutree/actions/workflows/tests.yml) [![codecov](https://codecov.io/github/mar10/nutree/branch/main/graph/badge.svg?token=9xmAFm8Icl)](https://codecov.io/github/mar10/nutree) [![License](https://img.shields.io/pypi/l/nutree.svg)](https://github.com/mar10/nutree/blob/main/LICENSE.txt) [![Documentation Status](https://readthedocs.org/projects/nutree/badge/?version=latest)](http://nutree.readthedocs.io/) [![Released with: Yabs](https://img.shields.io/badge/released%20with-yabs-yellowgreen)](https://github.com/mar10/yabs) [![StackOverflow: nutree](https://img.shields.io/badge/StackOverflow-nutree-blue.svg)](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/nutree) > _Nutree_ is a Python library for tree data structures with an intuitive, > yet powerful, API. **Nutree Facts** Handle multiple references of single objects ('clones')
Search by name pattern, id, or object reference
Compare two trees and calculate patches
Unobtrusive handling of arbitrary objects
Save as DOT file and graphwiz diagram
Nodes can be plain strings or objects
(De)Serialize to (compressed) JSON
Save as Mermaid flow diagram
Different traversal methods
Generate random trees
Convert to RDF graph
Fully type annotated
Typed child nodes
Pretty print
Navigation
Filtering
**Example** A simple tree, with text nodes ```py from nutree import Tree, Node tree = Tree("Store") n = tree.add("Records") n.add("Let It Be") n.add("Get Yer Ya-Ya's Out!") n = tree.add("Books") n.add("The Little Prince") tree.print() ``` ```ascii Tree<'Store'> ├─── 'Records' │ ├─── 'Let It Be' │ ╰─── "Get Yer Ya-Ya's Out!" ╰─── 'Books' ╰─── 'The Little Prince' ``` Tree nodes wrap the data and also expose methods for navigation, searching, iteration, ... ```py records_node = tree["Records"] assert isinstance(records_node, Node) assert records_node.name == "Records" print(records_node.first_child()) ``` ```ascii Node<'Let It Be', data_id=510268653885439170> ``` Nodes may be strings or arbitrary objects: ```py alice = Person("Alice", age=23, guid="{123-456}") tree.add(alice) # Lookup nodes by object, data_id, name pattern, ... assert isinstance(tree[alice].data, Person) del tree[alice] ``` [Read the Docs](https://nutree.readthedocs.io/) for more.

functions.name: nutree.common.check_python_version
documentation: Check for deprecated Python version.

functions.name: nutree.common.call_mapper
documentation:
Call the function and normalize result and exceptions.

Handles `MapperCallbackType`:
Call `fn(node, data)` if defined and return the result.
If `fn` is undefined or returns `None`, return `data`.

functions.name: nutree.common.call_predicate
documentation:
Call the function and normalize result and exceptions.

Handles `PredicateCallbackType`:
Call `fn(node)` and converts all raised
IterationControl responses to a canonical result.

functions.name: nutree.common.call_traversal_cb
documentation:
Call the function and handle result and exceptions.

This method calls `fn(node, memo)` and converts all returned or raised
IterationControl responses to a canonical result:

Handles `TraversalCallbackType`

- Return `False` if the method returns SkipBranch or an instance of
  SkipBranch.
- Raise `StopTraversal(value)` if the method returns False, StopTraversal, or an
  instance of StopTraversal.
- If a form of StopIteration is returned, we treat as StopTraversal, but
  emit a warning.
- Other return values are ignored and converted to None.

functions.name: nutree.common.open_as_uncompressed_input_stream
documentation:
Open a file for reading, decompressing if necessary.

Decompression is done by checking for the magic header (independent of the
file extension).

Example:

```
with open_as_uncompressed_stream("/path/to/foo.nutree") as fp:
    for line in fp:
        print(line)
```

functions.name: nutree.common.open_as_compressed_output_stream
documentation:
Open a file for writing, ZIP-compressing if requested.

Example:

```
with open_as_compressed_stream("/path/to/foo.nutree") as fp:
    fp:
        print(line)
```

classes.name: nutree.common.TreeError
documentation: Base class for all `nutree` errors.

classes.name: nutree.common.UniqueConstraintError
documentation: Thrown when trying to add the same node_id to the same parent

classes.name: nutree.common.AmbiguousMatchError
documentation: Thrown when a single-value lookup found multiple matches.

classes.name: nutree.common.IterMethod
documentation: Traversal order.

classes.name: nutree.common.IterationControl
documentation: Common base class for tree iteration controls.

classes.name: nutree.common.SkipBranch
documentation:
Raised or returned by traversal callbacks to skip the current node's descendants.

If `and_self` is true, some iterators will consider the node itself, but
still skip the descendants. For example {meth}`~nutree.tree.Tree.copy` and
{meth}`~nutree.tree.Tree.find_all`.
If `and_self` is false, some iterators will consider the node's children only.

classes.name: nutree.common.SelectBranch
documentation:
Raised or returned by traversal callbacks to unconditionally accept all
descendants.

classes.name: nutree.common.StopTraversal
documentation:
Raised or returned by traversal callbacks to stop iteration.

Optionally, a return value may be passed.
Note that if a callback returns `False`, this will be converted to a
`StopTraversal(None)` exception.

classes.name: nutree.common.DictWrapper

methods.name: nutree.common.DictWrapper.__setitem__
documentation:
Allow to access values as items.

Example:

```
`node.data["foo"] = 1` instead of `node.data._dict["foo"] = 1`.
```

methods.name: nutree.common.DictWrapper.__getitem__
documentation:
Allow to access values as items.

E.g. `foo = node.data["foo"]` instead of \`\` foo = node.data.\_dict["foo"]\`\`.

methods.name: nutree.common.DictWrapper.serialize_mapper
documentation:
Serialize the data object to a dictionary.

Example:

```
tree.save(file_path, mapper=DictWrapper.serialize_mapper)
```
def __getattr__(self, name: str) -> Any:
"""Allow to access values as attributes.
Assuming the DictWrapper instance is stored in a Node.data instance,
this allows to access the values like this::
node.data.NAME
If forward_attrs is enabled, this also allows to access the values like this::
node.NAME
See :ref:`generic-node-data`.
try:
return self._dict[name]
except KeyError:
raise AttributeError(name) from None

methods.name: nutree.common.DictWrapper.deserialize_mapper
documentation:
Serialize the data object to a dictionary.

Example:

```
tree = Tree.load(file_path, mapper=DictWrapper.deserialize_mapper)
```

Wrap a Python dict so it can be added to the tree.

Makes the dict hashable and comparable with `==`, so it can be used added to
the tree and can be checked for modifications during tree diffing.

Initialized with a dictionary of values. The values can be accessed
via the `node.data` attribute like `node.data["KEY"]`.

See {ref}`generic-node-data` for details.
Generic data object to be used when nutree.Node instances

methods.name: nutree.typed_tree.TypedNode.is_last_sibling

Args:

methods.name: nutree.typed_tree.TypedNode.add
documentation: Alias for {meth}`add_child`).

method signature again:

methods.name: nutree.typed_tree.TypedNode.append_child
documentation:
Append a new subnode.

This is a shortcut for {meth}`add_child` with `before=None`.

methods.name: nutree.typed_tree.TypedNode.prepend_child
documentation:
Prepend a new subnode.

This is a shortcut for {meth}`add_child` with `before=True`.

methods.name: nutree.typed_tree.TypedNode.prepend_sibling
documentation:
Add a new node **of same kind** before `self`.

This method calls {meth}`add_child` on `self.parent`.

methods.name: nutree.typed_tree.TypedNode.append_sibling
documentation:
Add a new node **of same kind** after `self`.

This method calls {meth}`add_child` on `self.parent`.

used by {class}`~nutree.typed_tree.TypedTree`.

In addition to {class}`~nutree.node.Node`, we have a `kind` member to
define the node's type.

modules.name: nutree
documentation:
Current version number.

See https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440

Examples
    Pre-releases (alpha, beta, release candidate):
        '3.0.0a1', '3.0.0b1', '3.0.0rc1'
    Final Release:
        '3.0.0'
    Developmental release (to mark 3.0.0 as 'used'. Don't publish this):
        '3.0.0.dev1'
NOTE:
    When pywin32 is installed, number must be a.b.c for MSI builds?
    "3.0.0a4" seems not to work in this case!

modules.name: nutree.tree_generator
documentation:
Implements a generator that creates a random tree structure from a specification.

See {ref}`randomize` for details.

functions.name: nutree.tree_generator._merge_specs
documentation: Tree Builder

functions.name: nutree.tree_generator.build_random_tree
documentation:
Return a nutree.TypedTree with random data from a specification.
See {ref}`randomize` for details.

classes.name: nutree.tree_generator.Randomizer
documentation:
Abstract base class for randomizers.

Args:
    probability (float, optional): The probability of using the randomizer.
        Must be in the range [0.0, 1.0]. Defaults to 1.0.
TTree = TypeVar("TTree", bound=Tree)
Randomizers

classes.name: nutree.tree_generator.RangeRandomizer
documentation:
A randomizer class that generates random values within a specified range.

Args:
    min_val (float| int): The minimum value of the range.
    max_val (float| int): The maximum value of the range.
    probability (float, optional): The probability of generating a value.
        Defaults to 1.0.
    none_value (Any, optional): The value to return when skipping generation.
        Defaults to None.

Returns:
    Union[float, int, None]: The generated random value, or none_value
        if generation is skipped.

classes.name: nutree.tree_generator.DateRangeRandomizer
documentation:
A randomizer class that generates random dates within a specified range.

Args:
    min_dt (date): The minimum date of the range.
    max_dt (date | int): The maximum date of the range.
        Pass an integer to specify the number of days from min_dt.
    as_js_stamp (bool, optional): If True, return the date as a JavaScript
        timestamp. Defaults to True.
    probability (float, optional): The probability of generating a value.
        Defaults to 1.0.
Examples:
    >>> DateRangeRandomizer(date(2020, 1, 1), date(2020, 12, 31)).generate()
    datetime.date(2020, 3, 7)
    >>> DateRangeRandomizer(date(2020, 1, 1), 365).generate()

classes.name: nutree.tree_generator.ValueRandomizer
documentation:
A randomizer class that generates a fixed value with a given probability.

Args:
    value (Any): The value to generate.
    probability (float): The probability of generating a value [0.0 .. 1.0].

classes.name: nutree.tree_generator.SparseBoolRandomizer
documentation:
A randomizer class that generates a boolean value with a given probability.
If the value is False, it is returned as None.

classes.name: nutree.tree_generator.SampleRandomizer
documentation: A randomizer class that generates a random value from a sample list.

classes.name: nutree.tree_generator.TextRandomizer
documentation:
A randomizer class that generates a random string value from a Fabulist template.

Uses the [`fabulist`](https://github.com/mar10/fabulist/) library to generate
text values.

Args:
    template (str | list): A template string or list of strings.
    probability (float, optional): The probability of generating a value.
        Defaults to 1.0.
class BoolRandomizer(SampleRandomizer):
def __init__(self, *, allow_none: bool = False) -> None:
if allow_none:
super().__init__((True, False, None))
else:
super().__init__((True, False))

classes.name: nutree.tree_generator.BlindTextRandomizer
documentation:
A randomizer class that generates a random lorem ipsum text value from a template.

Uses the [`fabulist`](https://github.com/mar10/fabulist/) library to generate
text values.

Args:
    sentence_count (int | tuple, optional): The number of sentences to generate.
        Defaults to (2, 6).
    dialect (str, optional): The dialect of the text. Defaults to "ipsum".
    entropy (int, optional): The entropy of the text. Defaults to 2.
    keep_first (bool, optional): If True, keep the first sentence.
        Defaults to False.
    words_per_sentence (int | tuple, optional): The number of words per sentence.
        Defaults to (3, 15).
    probability (float, optional): The probability of generating a value.
        Defaults to 1.0.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.__contains__
documentation: Implement `data in tree` syntax to check for node existence.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.__delitem__
documentation: Implement `del tree[data]` syntax to remove nodes.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.__enter__
documentation: Implement `with tree: ...` syntax to acquire an RLock.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.__getitem__
documentation:
Implement `tree[data]` syntax to lookup a node.

`data` may be a plain string, data object, data_id, or node_id.

Note: This is a flexible and concise way to access tree nodes. However,
{meth}`find_all` or {meth}`find_first` may be faster.

{class}`~nutree.common.AmbiguousMatchError` is raised if multiple matches
are found.
Use {meth}`find_all` or {meth}`find_first` instead to resolve this.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.__len__
documentation:
Make `len(tree)` return the number of nodes
(also makes empty trees falsy).

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.calc_data_id
documentation:
Called internally to calculate `data_id` for a `data` object.

This value is used to lookup nodes by data, identify clones, and for
(de)serialization. It defaults to `hash(data)` but may be overloaded
when the data objects have meaningful keys that should be used instead.

Note: By default, the \_\_hash\_\_() values of str and bytes objects are
'salted' with an unpredictable random value. Although they remain constant
within an individual Python process, they are not predictable between
repeated invocations of Python.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree._unregister
documentation: Unlink node from this tree (children must be unregistered first).

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.children
documentation:
Return list of direct child nodes, i.e. toplevel nodes
(list may be empty).

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.get_toplevel_nodes
documentation:
Return list of direct child nodes, i.e. toplevel nodes (may be
empty, alias for {meth}`children`).

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.count
documentation: Return the total number of nodes.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.count_unique
documentation:
Return the total number of `unique` nodes.

Multiple references to the same data object ('clones') are only counted
once.
This is different from {meth}`count`, which returns the number of `all`
nodes.

documentation: Change node's `data` and/or `data_id` and update bookkeeping.

Return a random node.

Note that there is also `IterMethod.RANDOM_ORDER`.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.calc_height
documentation: Return the maximum depth of all nodes.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.format
documentation:
Return a pretty string representation of the tree hierarchy.

See Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.format` method for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.print
documentation: Convenience method that simply runs print(self. {meth}`format()`).

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.add_child
documentation:
Add a toplevel node (same as shortcut {meth}`add`).

See Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.add_child` method for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.add
documentation:
Add a toplevel node (same as shortcut {meth}`add`).

See Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.add_child` method for details.
NOTE: mypy cannot handle this alias correctly, so we have to write the
method signature again:
#: Alias for :meth:`add_child`
add = add_child

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.copy
documentation:
Return a copy of this tree.

New {class}`Tree` and {class}`Node` instances are created.
The new nodes reference the original data objects.

`predicate` may be passed to filter the result, which is equivalent to
calling {meth}`~nutree.tree.Tree.filtered`

See Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.copy_to` and {ref}`iteration-callbacks`
method for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.copy_to
documentation:
Copy this tree's nodes to another target.

See Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.copy_to` method for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.filter
documentation:
In-place removal of unmatching nodes.

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.filtered
documentation:
Return a filtered copy of this tree.

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.clear
documentation: Remove all nodes from this tree.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.find_all
documentation:
Return a list of matching nodes (list may be empty).

See also Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.find_all` method and
{ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.find_first
documentation:
Return the first matching node or `None`.

Note that 'first' sometimes means 'one arbitrary' matching node, which
is not neccessarily the first of a specific iteration method.
See also Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.find_first` method and
{ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.sort
documentation:
Sort toplevel nodes (optionally recursively).

`key` defaults to `attrgetter("name")`, so children are sorted by
their string representation.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.to_dict_list
documentation:
Call Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.to_dict` method for all
child nodes and return list of results.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.from_dict
documentation:
Return a new {class}`Tree` instance from a list of dicts.

See also {meth}`~nutree.tree.Tree.to_dict_list` and
Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.find_first` methods, and
{ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.to_list_iter
documentation: Yield a parent-referencing list of child nodes.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.save
documentation:
Store tree in a compact JSON file stream.

If `target` is a string, it is interpreted as a file path. Otherwise it
must be a file object.

If `compression` is true, the file is compressed using gzip
(zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED).
Other values are: zipfile.ZIP_STORED, zipfile.ZIP_BZIP2, zipfile.ZIP_LZMA.
Pass False to disable compression and store as plain json.

See also {ref}`serialize` and {meth}`to_list_iter` and {meth}`load` methods.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.load
documentation:
Create a new {class}`Tree` instance from a file path or JSON file stream.

If `file_meta` is a dict, it receives the content if the file's
`meta` header.

See also {meth}`save`.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.to_dot
documentation:
Generate a DOT formatted graph representation.

See Node's {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.to_dot` method for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.to_dotfile
documentation:
Serialize a DOT formatted graph representation.

Optionally convert to a Graphviz display formats.
See {ref}`graphs` for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.to_mermaid_flowchart
documentation:
Serialize a Mermaid flowchart representation.

Optionally convert to a Graphviz display formats.
See {ref}`graphs` for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.to_rdf_graph
documentation:
Return an instance of `rdflib.Graph`.

See {ref}`graphs` for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.diff
documentation:
Compare this tree against `other` and return a merged, annotated copy.

The resulting tree contains a union of all nodes from this and the
other tree.
Additional metadata is added to the resulting nodes to classify changes
from the perspective of this tree. For example a node that only exists
in `other`, will have `node.get_meta("dc") == DiffClassification.ADDED`
defined.

The `compare` callback can be used to customize the comparison of nodes
It should return True if the nodes are considered equal, False otherwise.

If `ordered` is true, changes in the child order are also considered a
change. {{ br }}
If `reduce` is true, unchanged nodes are removed, leaving a compact tree
with only the modifications.

See {ref}`diff-and-merge` for details.

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree._self_check
documentation:
Internal method to check data structure sanity.

This is slow: only use for debugging, e.g. `assert tree._self_check()`.
def on(self, event_name: str, callback):
raise NotImplementedError

methods.name: nutree.tree.Tree.build_random_tree
documentation:
Build a random tree for .

Returns a new {class}`Tree` instance with random nodes, as defined by
structure_def.
If called like `TypedTree.build_random_tree(structure_def)`, this
method will return a {class}`~nutree.typed_tree.TypedTree` instance.

See {ref}`randomize` for details.

A Tree object is a shallow wrapper around a single, invisible system root node.
All visible toplevel nodes are direct children of this root node.
Trees expose methods to iterate, search, copy, filter, serialize, etc.

A `name` string can be passed for enhanced printing.

`calc_data_id` can be a callback function that calculates data IDs from data
objects (by default `hash(data)` is used).

Set `forward_attrs` to true, to enable aliasing of node attributes,
i.e. make `node.data.NAME` accessible as `node.NAME`. {{ br }}
**Note:** Use with care, see also {ref}`forward-attributes`.
- Tree

classes.name: nutree.node.Node

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.__init__
documentation: #: Default value for ``repr`` argument when formatting data for export,
#: like DOT, RDF, ...
DEFAULT_NAME_REPR = "{node.data!r}"
# DEFAULT_NAME_REPR = "{node.data}"

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.__eq__
documentation:
Implement `node == other` syntax to compare embedded data.

If `other` is a {class}`Node` instance, `self.data == other.data` is
evaluated.
Otherwise `self.data == other` is compared.

Use `node is other` syntax instead to check if two nodes are truly
identical.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.__getattr__
documentation:
Implement `node.NAME` aliasing to `node.data.NAME`.

See {ref}`forward-attributes`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.name
documentation: String representation of the embedded `data` object.
Do not define __len__: we don't want leaf nodes to evaluate as falsy
def __len__(self) -> int:
raise NotImplementedError("Use `len(node.data)` or `len(node._children)`.")

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.path
documentation: All ancestor names including self, starting with and separated by '/'.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.tree
documentation: Return container {class}`~nutree.tree.Tree` instance.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.parent
documentation:
Return parent node or None for toplevel nodes.

See also {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.up`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.up
documentation:
Return ancestor node.

Unlike {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.parent`, this method returns the
system root node for toplevel nodes.

One use case is method chaining when creating trees:

```
tree = Tree().add("n1").add("child1").up().add("child2").up(2).add("n2")
```

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.children
documentation: Return list of direct child nodes (list may be empty).

to modify).

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.data_id
documentation:
Return the wrapped data instance's id
(use {meth}`~nutree.tree.Tree.set_data()` to modify).

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.node_id
documentation: Return the node's unique key.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.meta
documentation:
Return the node's metadata dictionary or None if empty.

See also {meth}`get_meta`, {meth}`set_meta`, {meth}`update_meta`,
{meth}`clear_meta`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.get_meta
documentation: Return metadata value.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.set_meta
documentation: Set metadata value (pass value `None` to remove).

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.clear_meta
documentation: Reset all metadata or a distinct entry.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.update_meta

Add `values` dict to current metadata.

If `replace` is true, previous metatdata will be cleared.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.rename
documentation: Set `self.data` to a new string (assuming plain string node).

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.set_data
documentation: Change node's `data` and/or `data_id` and update bookkeeping.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.get_children
documentation: Return list of direct child nodes (list may be empty).
def set_data(
self,
data,
data_id: DataIdType | None = None,
with_clones: bool | None = None,
) -> None:
"""Change node's `data` and/or `data_id` and update bookkeeping."""
if not data and not data_id:
raise ValueError("Missing data or data_id")
tree = self.tree
if data is None or data is self._data:
new_data = None
else:
new_data = data
if data_id is None:
data_id = tree.calc_data_id(data)
if data_id is None or data_id == self._data_id:
new_data_id = None
else:
new_data_id = data_id
node_map = tree._nodes_by_data_id
cur_nodes = node_map[self._data_id]
has_clones = len(cur_nodes) > 1
if has_clones and with_clones is None:
raise AmbiguousMatchError(
"set_data() for clones requires `with_clones` decision"
if new_data_id:
# data_id (and possibly data) changes: we have to update the map
if has_clones:
if with_clones:
# Move the whole slot (but check if new id already exist)
prev_clones = node_map[self._data_id]
del node_map[self._data_id]
try:  # are we adding to existing clones now?
node_map[new_data_id].extend(prev_clones)
except KeyError:  # still a singleton, just a new data_id
node_map[new_data_id] = prev_clones
for n in prev_clones:
n._data_id = new_data_id
if new_data:
n._data = new_data
else:
# Move this one node to another slot in the map
node_map[self._data_id].remove(self)
try:  # are we adding to existing clones again?
node_map[new_data_id].append(self)
except KeyError:  # now a singleton with a new data_id
node_map[new_data_id] = [self]
self._data_id = new_data_id
if new_data:
self._data = new_data
else:
# data_id (and possibly data) changed for a *single* node
del node_map[self._data_id]
try:  # are we creating a clone now?
node_map[new_data_id].append(self)
except KeyError:  # still a singleton, just a new data_id
node_map[new_data_id] = [self]
self._data_id = new_data_id
if new_data:
self._data = new_data
elif new_data:
# `data` changed, but `data_id` remains the same:
# simply replace the reference
if with_clones:
for n in cur_nodes:
n._data = data
else:
self._data = new_data
return

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.first_child
documentation: First direct child node or None if no children exist.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.last_child
documentation: Last direct child node or None if no children exist.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.get_siblings

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.depth

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.count_descendants
documentation: Return number of descendant nodes, not counting self.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.calc_depth
documentation: Return the distance to the root node (1 for toplevel nodes).

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.calc_height
documentation: Return the maximum depth of all descendants (0 for leaves).

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.get_index
documentation: Return index in sibling list.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.is_system_root
documentation:
Return true if this node is the invisible system root
{class}`~nutree.tree._SystemRootNode`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.is_top
documentation: Return true if this node has no parent.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.is_leaf
documentation: Return true if this node is an end node, i.e. has no children.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.is_clone
documentation: Return true if this node's data is referenced at least one more time.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.is_first_sibling
documentation:
Return true if this node is the first sibling, i.e. the first child
of its parent.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.is_last_sibling
documentation:
Return true if this node is the last sibling, i.e. the last child
of its parent.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.has_children
documentation: Return true if this node has one or more children.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.get_top
documentation: Return toplevel ancestor (may be self).

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.is_descendant_of
documentation: Return true if this node is direct or indirect child of `other`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.is_ancestor_of
documentation: Return true if this node is a parent, grandparent, ... of `other`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.get_common_ancestor
documentation: Return the nearest node that contains `self` and `other` (may be None).

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.get_parent_list
documentation: Return ordered list of all parent nodes.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.get_path
documentation: Return a breadcrumb string, e.g. '/A/a1/a12'.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.add_child
documentation:
Append or insert a new subnode or branch as child.

If `child` is an existing {class}`~nutree.node.Node` instance, a copy
of this node will be created, that references the same `child.data`
object. {{ br }}
If `deep` is true, the children are copied recursively.

If `child` is a {class}`~nutree.tree.Tree`, all of its topnodes are
added recursively (unless `deep` is false).

If `child` is neither a {class}`~nutree.node.Node` nor a
{class}`~nutree.tree.Tree`, `child` itself will become the
`data` object of a new node that is added.

The source node may come from the same or from a foreign tree. {{ br }}
Note that adding the same data below one parent is not allowed.

If this node has no children yet, the new node is created as first child.
Otherwise, it will be appended to the existing children by default. {{ br }}
The `before` option may be used to specifiy the position:

> - False, None: append the new node as last child
> - True, 0: prepend the new node as first child
> - \<int>: prepend the new node before the existing child with this index
> - \<Node>: prepend the new node before this child node

Args:
: child (Node|Tree|TData):

  : Either an existing Node or a data object.

  before (bool|int|Node|None):

  : Optional position.

  deep (bool):

  : Copy descendants if any.
    This argument defaults to false when `child` is a
    {class}`~nutree.node.Node`.
    It defaults to true when `child` is a {class}`~nutree.tree.Tree`.

  data_id (str|int|None):

  : Allow to override the new node's `data_id`.
    This argument is only allowed for single nodes, but not for
    deep copies.
    Default `None` will to calculate from `hash(node.data)`.

  node_id (str|int|None):

  : Optional custom unique node key (defaults to `id(node)`)
    This argument is only allowed for single nodes, but not for
    deep copies.

Returns:

: the new {class}`~nutree.node.Node` instance

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.append_child
documentation:
Append a new subnode.

This is a shortcut for {meth}`add_child` with `before=None`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.prepend_child
documentation:
Prepend a new subnode.

This is a shortcut for {meth}`add_child` with `before=True`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.prepend_sibling
documentation:
Add a new node before `self`.

This method calls {meth}`add_child` on `self.parent`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.append_sibling
documentation:
Add a new node after `self`.

This method calls {meth}`add_child` on `self.parent`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.move_to
documentation:
Move this node to another parent.

By default, the node is appended to existing children.
See {meth}`add_child` for a description of `before`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.remove
documentation:
Remove this node.

If `keep_children` is true, all children will be moved one level up,
so they become siblings, before this node is removed.

If `with_clones` is true, all nodes that reference the same data
instance are removed as well.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.remove_children
documentation: Remove all children of this node, making it a leaf node.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.copy
documentation:
Return a new {class}`~nutree.tree.Tree` instance from this branch.

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.copy_to
documentation:
Copy this node to another parent and return the new node.

If `add_self` is set, a copy of this node becomes a child of `target`.
Otherwise copies of all children of this node are created below `target`.

By default new nodes are appended to existing children. The `before`
argument defines an alternative positioning.
It is only available when `add_self` is true.
See {meth}`add_child` for a description of `before`.

If `deep` is set, all descendants are copied recursively.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._add_from
documentation:
Append copies of all source descendants to self.

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._add_filtered
documentation:
Append a filtered copy of `other` and its descendants as children.

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.filtered
documentation:
Return a filtered copy of this node and descendants as tree.

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.filter
documentation:
In-place removal of mismatching nodes.

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.from_dict
documentation: Append copies of all source children to self.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._visit_pre
documentation: Depth-first, pre-order traversal.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._visit_post
documentation: Depth-first, post-order traversal.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._visit_level
documentation: Breadth-first (aka level-order) traversal.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.visit
documentation:
Call `callback(node, memo)` for all subnodes.

The callback may return {class}`SkipBranch` (or an instance
thereof) to omit child nodes but continue traversal otherwise.
Raising `SkipBranch` has the same effect.

The callback may return `False` or {class}`StopIteration` to immediately
interrupt traversal.
Raising `StopTraversal(value)` has the same effect but also allows to
specify a return value for the visit method. {{ br }}
See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

Args:
: callback (function(node, memo)):

  : Callback `function(node, memo)`

  add_self (bool):

  : If true, this node will also be visited (typically as first call).

  method (IterMethod):

  : Traversal method, defaults to pre-order, depth-first search.

  memo (Any):

  : This value will be passed to all calls and may be useful to
    implement caching or collect and return traversal results.
    If no `memo` argument is passed, an empty dict is created at
    start, which has a life-span of the traversal only.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._iter_pre
documentation: Depth-first, pre-order traversal.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._iter_post
documentation: Depth-first, post-order traversal.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._iter_level
documentation: Breadth-first (aka level-order) traversal.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._iter_level_rtl
documentation: Breadth-first (aka level-order) traversal, right-to-left.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._iter_zigzag
documentation: ZigZag traversal.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node._iter_zigzag_rtl
documentation: ZigZag traversal, right-to-left.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.iterator
documentation: Generator that walks the hierarchy.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.find_all
documentation:
Return a list of matching nodes (list may be empty).

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.find_first
documentation:
Return the first matching node or `None`.

See also {ref}`iteration-callbacks`.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.sort_children
documentation:
Sort child nodes.

`key` defaults to `attrgetter("name")`, so children are sorted by
their string representation.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.format_iter
documentation: This variant of {meth}`format` returns a line generator.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.format
documentation:
Return a pretty string representation of the node hierarchy.

more

Args:
    repr (str):
        A string or callback that defines how the node is rendered
        after the connector prefixes are generated.
    style (str):
        A string that defines the connector type, e.g. "round42" will
        produce "│ ", "╰─", "├─".
    add_self (bool):
        If false, only the descendants of this node are formatted.
    join (str):
        A string that is used to terminate the single lines. Defaults
        to "\n", but may be set to ", " together with `style="list"`
        to create a single line output.

Example:

    print(node.format(repr="{node.name}", style="round42"))

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.to_dict
documentation: Return a nested dict of this node and its children.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.to_list_iter
documentation:
Yield children as parent-referencing list.

```py
[(parent_key, data)]
```

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.to_dot
documentation:
Generate a DOT formatted graph representation.

See {ref}`graphs` for details.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.to_rdf_graph
documentation:
Return an instance of `rdflib.Graph`.

See {ref}`graphs` for details.

methods.name: nutree.node.Node.to_mermaid_flowchart
documentation:
Serialize a Mermaid flowchart representation.

Optionally convert to a Graphviz display formats.
See {ref}`graphs` for details.

A Node represents a single element in the tree.
It is a shallow wrapper around a user data instance, that adds navigation,
modification, and other functionality.

Node objects are rarely created using this contructor, but indirectly by
invoking helper methods like {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.add_child`, etc.

`data`

: is the arbitrary object that this node will hold.

`parent`

: is the parent {meth}`~nutree.node.Node` instance. This node will
  also inherit the tree reference from it.

`data_id`

: is an optional integer or string that will be used as ID
  (instead of calculating `hash(data)`).
  A tree may contain more than one node with the same data and data_id.
  In this case we call the nodes 'clones'.

`node_id`

: is an optional integer, that is used as unique ID for this node.
  Even 'clones' must have unique node IDs. The default is calculated as
  `id(self)`.

`meta`

: is an optional dictionary. See also {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.get_meta`,
  {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.set_meta`, {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.update_meta`,
  and {meth}`~nutree.node.Node.clear_meta`.
- Node
